rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF PERCY ORLANDO GREEN
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Order To Reconsider Order Appointing Bruce Edward Foggy As Administrator, Declaratoin Of Movant Joan Of Points And Authorities
rulings.Rulings: Part 1 of 2 This tentative ruling is issued by Judge Ross C. Moody. Before the Court is Joan Lynn Foggy's Motion for Order to Reconsider Order Appointing Bruce Edward Foggy as Administrator. The motion is DENIED. Joan Lynn Foggy and Bruce Foggy each filed competing petitions for letters of administration. Bruce filed an "Objection" to Joan's Petition and Joan filed an "Opposition" to Bruce's Petition. On July 5, 2023, the Court granted Bruce's Petition and appointed him administrator of the Estate of Percy Orlando Green. Bond was set at $1,600,000.00. Joan requests reconsideration of the appointment and moves for an order removing Bruce as administrator. Joan offers the following as facts or grounds in support of her request: (1) Judge Moody should have recused himself from the matter on the ground that Judge Moody is "biased"; (2) the Administrator has "no relation" to the Decedent or this case; (3) the Court "ignored" Joan Foggy and Darrell Brown's Petition for Letters of Administration, including Brown's nomination of Joan; and (4) Brown alone has the right to decide who oversees the administration of his brother's estate and whether bond should be waived. Joan further claims that Bruce, in his capacity as Administrator of the Estate of Booker T. Foggy, breached his fiduciary duties causing injury to Joan, and cites this as an additional basis for the Court to reconsider its order appointing Bruce Foggy as the Administrator of this Estate. Under Code of Civil Procedure section 1008, subdivision (a), "When an application for an order has been made to a judge, or to a court, and refused in whole or in part, or granted, or granted conditionally, or on terms, any party affected by the order may, within 10 days after service upon the party of written notice of entry of the order and based upon new or different facts, circumstances, or law, make an application to the same judge or court that made the order, to reconsider the matter and modify, amend, or revoke the prior order. The party making the application shall state by affidavit what application was made before, when and to what judge, what order or decisions were made, and what new or different facts, circumstances, or law are claimed to be shown." The court lacks the jurisdiction to reconsider a prior ruling, on motion of a party, where the motion does not comply with the requirements of Code of Civil Procedure section 1008. (Code Civ. Proc., 1008, subd. (e).) Section 1008's clear legislative intent is to "restrict motions to reconsider to circumstances where a party offers the court some fact or authority that was not previously considered by it" and could not have been considered by it. (Gilberd v. AC Transit (1995) 32 Cal.App.4th 1494, 1500.) There is also a strict requirement for diligence on the part of the moving party; the moving party must present a satisfactory explanation for failing to provide the evidence or different facts earlier. (Garcia v. Hejmadi (1997) 58 Cal.App.4th 674, 690.) As an initial matter, the motion is procedurally deficient. While Joan included a declaration as part of her motion, Joan failed to support the motion with a declaration or affidavit indicating what application was made before, when and to what judge, and what order or decisions were made. The Declaration of Joan L. Foggy contains none of this information. (end of part 1 see part 2)

rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF PERCY ORLANDO GREEN
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Motion For Order To Reconsider Order Appointing Bruce Edward Foggy As Administrator, Declaratoin Of Movant Joan Of Points And Authorities (Added For Posting Of The Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: Part 2 of 2 The motion is deficient in other ways. The motion does not comply with section 1008 in that it presents no new or different facts, circumstances, or law; nor is there any explanation provided for not offering any of the "new" or "different" facts earlier. To start, the identity of the judge who was hearing the competing petitions was known, or should have been known, to Joan prior to the July 5, 2023 hearing. At the very least, when Joan personally appeared in the courtroom she saw the judge Moody take the bench and call the instant matter. Rather than proceed with a challenge to Judge Moody, or otherwise raise her claims of bias, Joan proceeded with oral argument in front of him. The Administrator's relation to the Decedent was also known at the time of the hearing, as was Brown's nomination of Joan and Joan's allegations against Bruce in connection with his administration of the Estate of Booker T. Foggy. In fact, Joan referenced the alleged wrongful acts in great detail in her Opposition; and Bruce explained his relation to Decedent in his Objection, as well as in his own Petition. In ruling on the competing petitions, the Court considered Joan's Petition and Opposition, including Brown's nomination of Joan, Bruce's Petition and Objection, and the arguments of the parties at the hearing. Accordingly, none of these facts can serve as a basis for the Court to reconsider its order because they are neither "new" nor "different." To the extent Joan contends the Court should reconsider its appointment because "Brown alone" has the right to decide who oversees the administration of his brother's estate and whether bond should be waived, she offers no legal support for her proposition. Joan additionally requests the Court reconsider its July 5 order citing Code of Civil Procedure section 473. Neither subdivisions (a) or (b) of section 473 apply to an application for reconsideration of a trial court's order. Rather, section 1008 is the "exclusive means for modifying, amending or revoking an order. That limitation is expressly jurisdictional." (Gilberd v. AC Transit, supra, 32 Cal.App.4th at 1499; Code Civ. Proc., 1008, subd. (e).) For the foregoing reasons, the motion is denied. Administrator Bruce Foggy is ordered to lodge with the clerk in Department 204 a proposed order consistent with all substantive provisions of this tentative ruling, as published above, by the time set for this hearing. Any party wishing to contest this tentative ruling by appearing at the scheduled hearing must so advise other parties and the courtroom clerk (415-551-3702) no later than 4:00 p.m. on the court day before the hearing. Remote appearance is available consistent with San Francisco Superior Court Local Rules, rule 14.1(D). To appear by Zoom, go to zoom.us and click "Join Meeting." Enter Meeting ID number 1602254765 and passcode 514879. To appear telephonically through Zoom, call 1-669-900-6833 and follow the prompts. Enter Meeting ID number 1602254765# and passcode 514879#. Parties appearing via Zoom should use their full names in the "Your Name" section. Counsel and parties may appear by audio-only telephone through CourtCall by calling CourtCall at 1-888-882-6878 and obtaining an appearance access code for the hearing's scheduled date and time in Department 204. (end of tentative ruling)

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE KATHRYN LOFTUS 2017 LIVING TRUST ***TRANSFERRED TO SONOMA COUNTY***
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Notice Of Motion And Motion To Transfer Proceedings To Proper Venue And For Sanctions (Added To Calendar For Tentative Ruling Entry Purposes Only.)
rulings.Rulings: (Part 2 of 2 for purposes of entry of Tentative Ruling.) On this record, the Court finds that the day-to-day activity of the trust has been conducted in Sonoma County, not San Francisco, and for that reason Sonoma County is the principal place of administration for the instant trust. Because the Court is able to determine the principal place of administration under Probate Code section 17002(a), reference to section 17002(b) is unnecessary. For these reasons, transfer of the action to Sonoma County is appropriate. The Court declines to rule on the evidentiary objections raised by Sean Desmond regarding the material on pages 2 and 3 of the Memorandum of Points and Authorities, but alerts the parties that this material was not relevant to the decision on the motion to transfer, and was not relied on or credited in making this decision. All requests for sanctions are DENIED. Co-trustee Michael Desmond shall lodge with the clerk in Department 204 by the time set for this hearing a proposed order consistent with this tentative ruling as published above. Any party wishing to contest this tentative ruling by appearing at the scheduled hearing must so advise other parties and the courtroom clerk (415-551-3702) no later than 4:00 p.m. on the day before the hearing. To appear by Zoom, go to zoom.us and click "Join Meeting." Enter Meeting ID number 1602254765 and passcode 514879. To appear telephonically through Zoom, call 1-669-900-6833 and follow the prompts. Enter Meeting ID number 1602254765# and passcode 514879#. Parties appearing via Zoom should use their full names in the "Your Name" section. Counsel and parties may appear by audio-only telephone through CourtCall by calling CourtCall at 1-888-882-6878 and obtaining an appearance access code for the hearing's scheduled date and time in Department 204. (End of Tentative Ruling.)

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE KATHRYN LOFTUS 2017 LIVING TRUST ***TRANSFERRED TO SONOMA COUNTY***
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Transfer Proceedings To Proper Venue And For Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Tentative Ruling by Judge Ross C. Moody: (Part 1 of 2 for purposes of entry of Tentative Ruling.) Co-trustee Michael Desmond's Motion to Transfer Proceedings to Proper Venue and For Sanctions is GRANTED in part and DENIED in part. The proper venue for a proceeding regarding the internal affairs of a trust is the county of the trust's principal place of administration. (Prob. Code, Sec. 17005, subd. (a)(1).) Under Probate Code Sec. 17002, subdivision (a), "[t]he principal place of administration of the trust is the usual place where the day-to-day activity of the trust is carried on by the trustee or its representative who is primarily responsible for the administration of the trust." The statute goes on to instruct on how to proceed if the principal place of administration cannot be determined under subdivision (a). The statute indicates that "if" the court cannot determine where the principal place of administration is, then it should consider the residence of the co-trustees: "If the principal place of administration of the trust cannot be determined under subdivision (a), it shall be determined as follows: [] ... [] (2) If the trust has more than one trustee, the principal place of administration of the trust is the residence or usual place of business of any of the co-trustees as agreed upon by them or, if not, the residence or usual place of business of any of the co-trustees." (Prob. Code, Sec. 17002, subd. (b)(2).) The moving party asks the Court to take judicial notice of his verified petition to determine the terms of the trust filed August 17, 2023 in this case. He notes that in paragraphs 20-22 of that petition, he alleges the following: "The day-to-day activities relating to the administration of the Trust were conducted from Michael's home in Santa Rosa, and all of the records of the Trust have been stored there. That continues to this very day. Michael has engaged in extensive activities relating to the renovation of the settlor's home in preparation for its contemplated sale. Michael took these actions from his home in Santa Rosa and all of the relevant records continue to be stored there." These assertions are verified under penalty of perjury, and the Court is entitled to rely on them. The assertions demonstrate that the day-to-day activity of the trust has been carried on by co-trustee Michael Desmond in Sonoma County, not in San Francisco. In contrast, co-trustee Sean Desmond's declaration opposing the motion to transfer is silent regarding what day-to-day activity of the trust he has engaged in in San Francisco. Instead, he lists the actions he "intends" to take in San Francisco with respect to the trust in the future: "As cotrustee I intend to exercise my powers and fiduciary duties for the benefit of the beneficiaries of the trust. I intend to take the following actions in cooperation with Michael Desmond in order to protect the trust assets: 1. Obtain the Trust EIN, open a trust account, protect the trust real property, confirm real property insurance coverage, pay any bills, debts, taxes, prepare the final 1040, 540 individual returns, verify prior year tax returns filed, obtain original death certificate, prepare Affidavit of Death of Trustee, and Certification of Trust; 2. Protect the real property, and list the real property for sale; 3. Inventory assets, properly maintain and manage books and records. 4. Timely make specific pecuniary distributions to the beneficiaries." (Declaration of Sean Desmond, paragraph 19.) (Tentative Ruling continues in Part 2 of 2.)
